content.full_text:
REMCHO, JOHANSEN & PURCELL
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

220 MONTGOMERY STREET, SUITE 800
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104
415/ 398-6230    FAX: 415/ 398-7256


July 13, 1992


Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

Re: Assemblywoman Barbara Lee

Dear Commission:

     I am writing on behalf of Assemblywoman Barbara Lee
to request assistance with respect to the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
Assemblywoman Lee has authorized my law firm to represent her
and to make this request on her behalf.

     Barbara Lee is a member of the California Assembly.
Assemblywoman Lee is a member of the Assembly Education
Committee, but not the Higher Education Committee.
Assemblywoman Lee is also the owner of Lee Associates, a
personnel consulting firm.  Lee Associates wants to enter
into contracts with the University of California to provide
personnel services at the Lawrence Livermore National
Laboratories.  Assemblywoman Lee requests assistance
regarding the application of the Act to decisions involving
the University of California.

     In particular, do the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act, including the Act's
administrative remedies, apply to Assemblywoman Lee's
participating in or attempting to influence decisions
involving the University of California, the Lawrence
Livermore National Laboratory, or any branches of the
University of California?

     It is our understanding that under the new conflict
of interest laws that became effective January 1, 1991, the
administrative, civil, or criminal remedies of the Act apply
to members of the Legislature only under limited
circumstances. The laws apply only to legislation that is

Fair Political Practices Commission
July 13, 1992
Page 2


"nongeneral legislation." "Nongeneral legislation" is
defined as legislation: 1) that is not of a general nature
as defined by section 16 of article IV of the California
Constitution, Gov. Code § 87102.5(b)(4)); and 2) for which
"[i]t is reasonably foreseeable that [it] will have direct
and significant financial impact on one or more identifiable
persons, or one or more identifiable pieces of real
property," but where it is not "reasonably foreseeable that
the legislation will have a similar impact on the public
generally or on a significant segment of the public." Gov.
Code § 87102.6(a). "More than a small number of persons or
pieces of real property" constitutes a "significant segment
of the public." Gov. Code § 87102.6(b)(5).

The following specific questions arise:

A. Last year, you provided Assemblywoman Lee
assistance regarding decisions involving the California
Department of Transportation, a former source of income to
her. See FPPC Informal Assistance Letter I-90-719. You
concluded that Assemblywoman Lee would likely not have
conflicts of interest with respect to legislative decisions
affecting the Department of Transportation because the
decisions affected "the public generally or a significant
segment of the public in a similar manner." In so finding,
you observed that "generally, the effect of a legislative
decision on a state agency flows to all residents of the
state." Id. at 5.

Under analogous reasoning, will Assemblywoman Lee
not have conflicts of interest with respect to legislative
decisions affecting the University of California, the
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, and any of the
branches of the University of California?

(1) Does legislation that has a material
effect on the University of California,
the Lawrence Livermore National
Laboratory, or any of the branches of the
University of California affect all
residents of the state or a significant
segment of the residents of the state and
thus have a similar effect on the public
generally or a significant segment of the
public?

Fair Political Practices Commission
July 13, 1992
Page 3


                    (2)  Does legislation that has a material
                effect on the University of California,
                the Lawrence Livermore National
                Laboratory, or any of the branches of the
                University of California have a similar
                impact on "more than a small number of
                persons?"

                    (3)  Is legislation that has a material
                effect on the University of California,
                the Lawrence Livermore National
                Laboratory, or any of the branches of the
                University of California of a general
                nature as defined by the California
                Constitution or otherwise not nongeneral
                legislation as defined by the Act?

        B.  If Assemblywoman Lee could have a conflict of
interest with respect to some decisions affecting the
University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National
Laboratory, or one of the branches of the University of
California, Assemblywoman Lee would have a conflict of
interest with respect to the following bills:

                    (1)  AB 3320 - Regents of the University
                of California:  meetings.  The
                Legislative Counsel's Digest describes
                the bill in pertinent part as follows:

                         Existing law authorizes the Regents
                    of the University of California to
                    conduct closed sessions when meeting
                    to consider or discuss, among other
                    things, matters concerning the
                    appointment, employment,
                    performance, compensation, or
                    dismissal of university officers or
                    employees, as specified.

                         This bill would delete the authority
                    of the regents to conduct closed
                    sessions when they meet to consider
                    the compensation of university
                    officers or employees. . . .

Fair Political Practices Commission
July 13, 1992
Page 4


The bill also would express the
intent of the Legislature that no
proposal relating to . . .
compensation paid to an officer of
the university . . . shall become
effective unless disclosure is made
to each regent and the public, as
specified, and the regents approve
the proposal by a majority vote of
the membership of the regents.

(2) A bill that affected only the
Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory.

(3) A bill that affected only one branch
of the University of California.

C. Other than decisions discussed above, can you
identify any types of decisions that would create a conflict
of interest for Assemblywoman Lee?

We appreciate your prompt attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

[signature]
John A. Lewis

JAL:PJA
cc: The Honorable Barbara Lee

sections.question:
In particular, do the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act, including the Act's administrative remedies, apply to Assemblywoman Lee's participating in or attempting to influence decisions involving the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or any branches of the University of California?

The following specific questions arise:

A. Under analogous reasoning [to FPPC Informal Assistance Letter I-90-719], will Assemblywoman Lee not have conflicts of interest with respect to legislative decisions affecting the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, and any of the branches of the University of California?

(1) Does legislation that has a material effect on the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or any of the branches of the University of California affect all residents of the state or a significant segment of the residents of the state and thus have a similar effect on the public generally or a significant segment of the public?

(2) Does legislation that has a material effect on the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or any of the branches of the University of California have a similar impact on "more than a small number of persons"?

(3) Is legislation that has a material effect on the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or any of the branches of the University of California of a general nature as defined by the California Constitution or otherwise not nongeneral legislation as defined by the Act?

B. If Assemblywoman Lee could have a conflict of interest with respect to some decisions affecting the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or one of the branches of the University of California, would Assemblywoman Lee have a conflict of interest with respect to: (1) AB 3320 - Regents of the University of California: meetings; (2) A bill that affected only the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory; (3) A bill that affected only one branch of the University of California.

C. Other than decisions discussed above, can you identify any types of decisions that would create a conflict of interest for Assemblywoman Lee?
sections.question_synthetic: Does Assemblywoman Barbara Lee, owner of a personnel consulting firm, have conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act's nongeneral legislation provisions when participating in decisions affecting the University of California, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or UC branches? Specifically, does legislation affecting these entities have a similar impact on the public generally or a significant segment of the public, and would specific bills create conflicts of interest?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion section present. Document contains detailed question section with multiple sub-questions (A, B, C) regarding applicability of nongeneral legislation conflict of interest rules. References prior FPPC letter I-90-719 regarding Assemblywoman Lee and Caltrans. OCR quality is generally good with minor scanning artifacts.

The following specific questions arise:

A. Under analogous reasoning [to FPPC Informal Assistance Letter I-90-719], will Assemblywoman Lee not have conflicts of interest with respect to legislative decisions affecting the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, and any of the branches of the University of California?

(1) Does legislation that has a material effect on the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or any of the branches of the University of California affect all residents of the state or a significant segment of the residents of the state and thus have a similar effect on the public generally or a significant segment of the public?

(2) Does legislation that has a material effect on the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or any of the branches of the University of California have a similar impact on "more than a small number of persons"?

(3) Is legislation that has a material effect on the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or any of the branches of the University of California of a general nature as defined by the California Constitution or otherwise not nongeneral legislation as defined by the Act?

B. If Assemblywoman Lee could have a conflict of interest with respect to some decisions affecting the University of California, the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, or one of the branches of the University of California, would Assemblywoman Lee have a conflict of interest with respect to: (1) AB 3320 - Regents of the University of California: meetings; (2) A bill that affected only the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory; (3) A bill that affected only one branch of the University of California.

C. Other than decisions discussed above, can you identify any types of decisions that would create a conflict of interest for Assemblywoman Lee?

embedding.summary: Attorney letter requesting FPPC assistance on behalf of Assemblywoman Barbara Lee regarding whether conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to her legislative decisions involving the University of California and Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, given her ownership of a personnel consulting firm seeking UC contracts.
